claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 10 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 2 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 2 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 8 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 10 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin. To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I first need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**First question: How old is the child?**

The age of the child is important because:
- Children **under 16** may automatically qualify
- Young people **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Those **aged 20 or over** generally do not qualify

Please tell me the age of the child (or children) you want to claim for.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm interested in finding out if I can claim Child Benefit. I have three children I'd like to ask about - Alex is 10, Blake is 19, and Charlie is 2 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can help you assess eligibility for all three of your children: Alex (10), Blake (19), and Charlie (2).

Let me work through each child systematically, as they fall into different age categories with different requirements.

## Starting with Alex (age 10):

Since Alex is under 16, let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (age 10):** Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?

(For a 10-year-old, this would typically be "yes" - they're not working full-time)

Then I'll need to assess Charlie (age 2) and Blake (age 19) as well. Blake's case will be particularly important since at 19, there are specific education/training requirements to meet.

Could you confirm:
1. **Alex (10)** - Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week? (I expect yes)
2. **Charlie (2)** - Same question for Charlie - working less than 24 hours per week? (I expect yes)
3. **Blake (19)** - This is important: Is Blake currently in approved education or training? (This will determine Blake's eligibility)

Let me know these details and we'll continue through the assessment!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Alex and Charlie - no, they're not working at all, they're just kids!

As for Blake, no, he's not in any education or training at the moment. He finished school a while back and he's just at home with me right now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying! Let me continue the assessment for each child.

## Alex (age 10) - Not working:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (10) and Charlie (2):** Are they receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

(This would be unusual for young children, but I need to confirm)

## For Blake (age 19):

Since Blake is not currently in approved education or training, this is a key eligibility issue. Let me check the specific pathway for 16-19 year olds not in education.

Let me gather a bit more information:

**Questions:**
1. **Alex and Charlie:** Are they receiving ESA or Universal Credit in their own right? (I expect no)
2. **Blake:** When did Blake leave education or training? Was it recently, or has it been some time?

The reason I'm asking about Blake is that there may be a brief extension period that applies in certain circumstances, so the timing matters.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No, Alex and Charlie aren't getting any benefits themselves - they're just children.

As for Blake, he left school quite a while ago now - it's been well over a year since he finished.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me continue the assessment for Alex and Charlie, and then address Blake's situation.

## Alex (age 10) - Not receiving benefits:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (10) and Charlie (2):** Are either of them in any type of apprenticeship?

(Again, for children this young, the answer would typically be no)

## For Blake (age 19):

Since Blake left school over a year ago and is not currently in approved education or training, unfortunately **Blake would not qualify** as a qualifying child for Child Benefit purposes. The rules are clear that 16-19 year olds must be in approved education or training to qualify, and there are only short extension periods (like over summer holidays) that might apply.

Let me complete the assessment for Alex and Charlie, then I'll give you the full picture for all three children.

**Quick confirmation:** Are Alex and Charlie in any apprenticeships? (I expect no)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - Alex is only 10 and Charlie is just 2! They're not in any apprenticeships.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: You're absolutely right - that was a bit of an obvious question! Let me continue with the assessment for Alex and Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we're getting to the questions about **you** as the claimant.

**Residency question:** Do you meet the UK residency requirements? 

You would qualify if **ANY** of these apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common situation)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working/studying/job seeking, etc.
4. You're a Crown servant living abroad

Do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Responsibility for the children:** Do you live with Alex, Blake, and Charlie, or are you financially responsible for them?

You would qualify if **ANY** of these apply:
1. **You live with the children** (most common)
2. You contribute to their upkeep (paying for food, clothes, housing, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Do you live with Alex, Blake, and Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live with Alex and Blake - they're both at home with me. But Charlie doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is important - we'll need to assess Alex and Blake separately from Charlie.

**For Alex and Blake** (who live with you): You meet the responsibility requirement since you live with them.

**For Charlie** (who doesn't live with you): Do you contribute financially to Charlie's upkeep? This could include:
- Paying for food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents
- Making maintenance payments under a court order or agreement
- Any contributions that equal or exceed the Child Benefit amount

Are you contributing financially to Charlie's care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Not at the moment, no. I'm not able to contribute anything financially for Charlie right now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for being honest about that. That's an important detail for Charlie's eligibility.

Let me continue the assessment for Alex and Blake (who you live with):

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex and Blake:** Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?

For example:
- Another parent
- A grandparent or other family member
- Anyone else who might have submitted a claim

Are you the only person claiming (or planning to claim) Child Benefit for Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm the only one claiming for Alex and Blake. Nobody else is getting Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent. Let me continue the assessment for Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex and Blake:** Are either of them currently:
- In hospital?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or are they living normally at home with you?

What's the living situation for Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex has been in local authority care for a couple of months now, but Blake is just living at home with me normally.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. This is very important - if Alex has been in local authority care, this affects eligibility.

Let me check the specific rules about children in care:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex in care:** You mentioned Alex has been in local authority care for a couple of months. This is a critical point because:

- Child Benefit can continue for **up to 8 weeks** when a child first goes into care
- After 8 weeks, you generally can't claim unless the child spends **at least 24 hours per week at home** with you

Since Alex has been in care for "a couple of months" (which sounds like it's more than 8 weeks), the key question is:

**Does Alex spend at least 24 hours per week at home with you?** 

For example, does Alex come home on weekends or visit regularly?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex comes home to stay with me every weekend.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know! Let me ask for a bit more detail:

**Does Alex spend at least 24 hours per week with you during these weekend visits?**

For example, if Alex comes home Friday evening and stays until Sunday evening, that would typically be more than 24 hours. The rule specifically requires at least 24 hours per week at home for you to continue claiming Child Benefit after the initial 8-week period.

Can you confirm Alex is with you for 24 hours or more each week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, definitely - when Alex comes home for the weekend, it's easily more than 24 hours. Usually Friday evening through to Sunday.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! That means you can continue to claim Child Benefit for Alex even though they're in care, because Alex spends at least 24 hours per week at home with you.

Let me continue the assessment for both Alex and Blake:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now a very important question:

**For Alex:** Since Alex is in local authority care, is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance costs?

**For Blake:** Is the local council paying anything towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance?

(The council paying for the child's care typically disqualifies Child Benefit claims)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Alex, yes, the local authority is covering the costs while Alex is in care.

For Blake, no - the council isn't paying anything for Blake. He's just living at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarification. This is crucial information.

**For Alex:** Unfortunately, because the local council is paying for Alex's accommodation and maintenance while in care, you **cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex** - even though Alex spends weekends with you. The rule is that Child Benefit isn't available when the council is covering the child's costs.

**For Blake:** Since the council is not paying anything for Blake and he's living normally at home with you, let me navigate to check his eligibility:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great news for Blake! Now let me get some additional information to complete the assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Now let me provide you with the complete assessment for all three children:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
# Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Complete Results

I've completed the assessment for all three of your children. Unfortunately, **you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for any of them** at this time. Here's the breakdown:

---

## **Alex (age 10) - NOT ELIGIBLE** ❌

Alex meets most of the requirements:
- ✅ Under 16 years old
- ✅ Not working full-time
- ✅ Not receiving benefits in their own right
- ✅ You have responsibility (Alex spends 24+ hours per week at home with you)
- ✅ You meet UK residency requirements

**However:** The local council is paying for Alex's accommodation and maintenance while in care. This is a disqualifying factor - you cannot claim Child Benefit when the council is covering the child's costs, even though Alex comes home on weekends.

---

## **Blake (age 19) - NOT ELIGIBLE** ❌

Blake is in the 16-19 age category, which has special requirements:
- ✅ Lives with you
- ✅ You meet residency requirements
- ❌ **Not in approved education or training**

Since Blake left school over a year ago and is not currently in any approved education or training, he does not qualify as a "qualifying child" for Child Benefit. Young people aged 16-19 must be in approved education or training (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study) to be eligible.

---

## **Charlie (age 2) - NOT ELIGIBLE** ❌

Charlie meets the age requirement (under 16), but:
- ❌ **You do not live with Charlie**
- ❌ **You are not contributing financially to Charlie's upkeep**

To claim Child Benefit, you must either live with the child OR contribute financially to their care (at least the equivalent of Child Benefit payments). Since neither applies, you cannot claim for Charlie.

---

## **What You Could Do:**

**For Alex:** If the local council stops paying for Alex's care and Alex returns home full-time, you would then be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

**For Blake:** If Blake enrolls in approved education or training (A-levels, BTEC, NVQ Level 1-3, etc. - at least 12 hours per week), you could then claim Child Benefit for him until he turns 20.

**For Charlie:** If you start living with Charlie or begin contributing financially to Charlie's upkeep (at an amount equal to or exceeding the Child Benefit payment), you could then claim.

---

I understand this isn't the outcome you were hoping for. If any of these circumstances change, you may become eligible in the future. If you have any questions or need clarification, please let me know.

reasonings: Alex is 10 years old and meets the age requirement (under 16). Alex is not working full-time and is not receiving benefits in their own right. You live in the UK and have responsibility for Alex through the weekend visits (spending 24+ hours per week at home). However, Alex is in local authority care and the local council is paying for Alex's accommodation and maintenance. Under Child Benefit rules, you cannot claim Child Benefit when the local council is covering the child's costs, even though Alex spends weekends at home with you. Therefore, Alex does not qualify.

Blake is 19 years old, which falls into the 16-19 age category that requires the young person to be in approved education or training to qualify. Blake is not currently in any education or training, having left school over a year ago. Blake is not in the extension period (which only applies immediately after leaving education, typically over summer). Since Blake does not meet the education/training requirement for 16-19 year olds, Blake does not qualify as a qualifying child for Child Benefit purposes.

Charlie is 2 years old and meets the age requirement (under 16). However, you indicated that Charlie does not live with you and you are not currently able to contribute anything financially to Charlie's upkeep. Under Child Benefit rules, you must either live with the child OR contribute financially to their care (at least the equivalent of Child Benefit payments). Since you do not meet the responsibility requirement for Charlie, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children but unfortunately does not qualify for Child Benefit for any of them. Alex (10) would otherwise qualify but is disqualified because the local council is paying for their care. Blake (19) does not qualify because he is not in approved education or training as required for 16-19 year olds. Charlie (2) does not qualify because the claimant does not live with Charlie and is not contributing financially to Charlie's upkeep, failing the responsibility requirement.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

You live in the UK and have responsibility for Alex through the weekend visits (spending 24+ hours per week at home). However, Alex is in local authority care and the local council is paying for Alex's accommodation and maintenance. Under Child Benefit rules, you cannot claim Child Benefit when the local council is covering the child's costs, even though Alex spends weekends at home with you. Therefore, Alex does not qualify.

Blake is not in the extension period (which only applies immediately after leaving education, typically over summer). Since Blake does not meet the education/training requirement for 16-19 year olds, Blake does not qualify as a qualifying child for Child Benefit purposes.

Charlie is 2 years old and meets the age requirement (under 16). However, you indicated that Charlie does not live with you and you are not currently able to contribute anything financially to Charlie's upkeep. Since you do not meet the responsibility requirement for Charlie, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Charlie.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children but unfortunately does not qualify for Child Benefit for any of them. Alex (10) would otherwise qualify but is disqualified because the local council is paying for their care. Blake (19) does not qualify because he is not in approved education or training as required for 16-19 year olds. Charlie (2) does not qualify because the claimant does not live with Charlie and is not contributing financially to Charlie's upkeep, failing the responsibility requirement.
